images.caption: Figure 1:OurXX-Stream, as the first multi-stream streaming benchmark, encompasses a diverse range of scenarios featuring multi-angle, multi-view, and multi-device capabilities.andmean balanced and imbalanced streams.andmean the same domain and different domain streams.andmean the real-world and synthesized pairs.

Figure 2:The illustration of the multi-streaming task. Fig.(a) and (b) showcase the practical examples in daily life.
Essentially, the multi-streaming task involves multiple videos with temporal constraints and alignment, requiring the synchronization of video timestamps, as shown in Fig.(c). However, compared to multi-view and multi-angle, it also necessitates important streaming properties to fit the online applications.
Figure 3:The illustration of the 4 multi-stream abilities. To evaluate these abilities, ourXX-Streaming Benchmark includes 3 progressive dimensions and 11 subtasks.

Figure 5:MLLMs can only handle one token stream at a time, making a multiplexer essential for integrating multiple video streams into one token stream. To address this, we investigate three multiplexing strategies and uncover their inherent trade-offs. During evaluation, the model sequentially processes continuous video streams in 1-second intervals while maintaining a sliding memory window for context management.

Table 2:Comprehensive streaming performance comparison of mLLMs on the X-Stream Benchmark (Ours). The symbols “” and “” indicate video and audio support, respectively. Background colors denote the top three results within each scene:green (1st),blue (2nd), andyellow (3rd). Among open-source models,boldandunderlinedhighlight the best and second-best.

Table 3:Comprehensive performance comparison of MLLMs across 3 multi-stream dimensions and 11 core tasks onXX-Stream Benchmark.
The “∗*” symbol indicates that the model lacks audio capabilities and is evaluated directly on the question, while other notations follow Tab.2.
Figure 6:The case study in ourXX-Stream Benchmark. We choose a 4-stream, proactive, free-form QA (yellow) and a 2-stream, proactive, multi-choice QA (green) as examples.
Figure A7:Data Preview. This preview highlights the main real-world multi-stream applications and offers an overview of the diversity of ourXX-Stream.
Figure B8:Distribution of the original data (before processing).
Figure B9:Distribution of the original data (before processing).
Figure B11:Word cloud of the free-form answers (after processing).
Table B12:Human correction statistics on the evaluation set.
Figure B13:Error type distribution on the human evaluation.

Figure D14:The difference in Grid Raster-scan order causes a performance gap.
In general, raster-scanning can be understood as a left-to-right, top-to-bottom process.
For horizontal concatenation, scanning typically results in tokens from multiple streams being interleaved within the same frame.
Conversely, vertical concatenation generally prevents tokens from multiple streams from interleaving during scanning.
Regardless of the method used, however, a certain amount of overlapping tokens is inevitable.
